messages.content: You digest raw market-research search results into citable claim atoms for a risk-hunting pass over a market model. Extract only what the text actually says — every excerpt must be verbatim. Numeric claims are worth more than qualitative ones.

THE CURATED LEDGER NODES THIS GROUP FEEDS (with their derivations — compare against them):

  "derivation": {
   "method": "Top-down capacity apportionment",
   "expression": "PL colo 228 MW ÷ CE-7 colo 3,451 MW (Statista 2025) = 0.066 → 0.07 (normalized)",
   "crossCheck": "Installed-IT-load method (Mordor 660 MW, 2025) → ~0.10 — scopes diverge, band widened"
  },
  "provenance": {
   "rationale": "Colo vs installed-load scopes diverge ~1.5x for the fast-growing Polish base → inferred, wide band.",
   "promotionPath": "Adopt one scope (PMR commercial-DC series) across all CE countries → narrow band."
  },

# Poland

Supply: 605 MW

Total Supply is the sum of four supply categories: Live, Under Construction, Committed, and Early Stage.

Facilities: 69

## Overview

The Polish data centre market has historically consisted of retail colocation facilities occupied mainly by enterprises. Nowadays, we are seeing a shift towards a wholesale colocation market that is driven mainly by accelerated cloud adoption by enterprises and the public sector.

The market has grown significantly in the past four years, going from 184 MW in 2020 to 481 MW of total capacity in 2024 with most of the growth occurring in Warsaw. Supply and demand has remained consistent, with the highest uptick of supply noted in 2022 driven by the entrance of major international players in the market. Poland's central location in Europe as well as its proximity to major established and emerging markets, and its potential for renewable energy makes it an attractive hub for data centre development.

     "publishedDate": "2026-05-22T16:02:08.000Z",
     "text": "Poland's Data Center Power Demand to 2030: What Microsoft and Google's Investments Mean for Investors - Prime East - Real Estate Investment and Development Boutique

# Poland’s Data Center Power Demand to 2030: What Microsoft and Google’s Investments Mean for Investors

22 May 2026

Central and Eastern Europe’s digital map is being redrawn around one country. Poland has moved from a colocation afterthought to the region’s fastest-growing data center hub and the question that now defines the investment case is no longer whether demand will arrive, but whether the power to serve it can be delivered in time.

With Microsoft and Google anchoring multi-billion-zloty commitments and AI workloads compounding the load, the gap between data center demand and available grid capacity is becoming the single most important variable for investors and operators looking at Poland through 2030.

## How big is Poland’s data center power demand by 2030?

The headline numbers point in one direction: up, and fast. Poland’s installed data center IT load is forecast to grow from roughly 660 MW in 2025 to between 930 and 1,000 MW by 2030 a compound annual growth rate of around 7% on the conservative measure. On the capital-spend side the picture is steeper still: market researcher Arizton values the Polish data center market at USD 1.16 billion in 2024 and projects USD 2.78 billion by 2030, a CAGR near 15.7%. Warsaw alone is expected to expand capacity by roughly 67% cumulatively over 2025–2030.

Th"

     "title": "Poland's Data Center Boom: 3.2GW, Below-€10M Builds, and Europe's Largest Energy Construction Site - datacenterHawk",
     "url": "https://datacenterhawk.com/resources/hawktalks/poland-s-data-center-boom-3-2gw-below-10m-builds-and-europe-s-largest-energy-construction-site",
     "publishedDate": null,
     "text": "Poland's Data Center Boom: 3.2GW, Below-€10M Builds, and Europe's Largest Energy Construction Site - datacenterHawk

### Poland's Data Center Market: 3.2GW Projects, Below-€10M Builds, and Europe's Largest Energy Transformation

Poland has moved from a peripheral mention to a central topic in European data center investment conversations. In a conversation with datacenterHawk at Gateway Poland 2026 in Warsaw, Piotr Kowalski, President of the Polish Data Center Association, laid out the data behind a market that has tripled in capacity since 2020 and is now attracting gigawatt-scale development interest.

### From 70MW to Gigawatt-Scale Ambitions

Poland's data center capacity has grown from approximately 70MW in 2020 to 220MW today, driven primarily by the arrival of all three major hyperscalers. Microsoft is building its own data center in the country, while Google and AWS opened cloud regions through colocation facilities. That initial wave created an ecosystem that now includes operators like Atman, Beyond, Equinix, Data4 Group, Switch Data Centers, and Budi Max (part of Ferrovial Group), along with EdgeConneX and Vantage Data Centers. Market projections point to 500-600MW by 2030, roughly three times current capacity. But the real headline is in the development pipeline. Three projects of unusual scale have been announced: a 500MW development in central Poland, a 950MW project in Western Pomerania (150km from Berlin), and a 3.2GW opportunity on Poland's north coast near t"

# Poland Data Center Market Size, Share, and Industry Trends Forecast 2026-2036

The Poland Data Center Market valued at $2.8 Billion in 2026 is forecast to scale to $9.55 Billion by 2035, progressing at a 14.60% CAGR throughout the forecast period. Cloud service providers and financial institutions are expanding their footprint in Warsaw and Krakow. The Polish government's National Cloud Strategy and EU Green Deal compliance requirements are accelerating facility modernization. Demand concentrates in the Warsaw metropolitan corridor, where fiber density and power grid capacity support large-scale deployments.

The Poland data center market is expected to grow from USD 1.89 billion in 2025 to USD 2.17 billion in 2026 and is forecast to reach USD 4.29 billion by 2031 at 14.63% CAGR over 2026-2031.Strong hyperscale cloud investments, rapid enterprise digitalization, and a growing renewable-energy pipeline anchor the expansion of the Poland data center market. In parallel, the nation’s installed IT load capacity is forecast to rise from 660 MW in 2025 to 930 MW in 2030, a 7.34% CAGR that signals a pivot toward AI-optimized, high-density deployments rather than pure footprint growth. The market segment shares and estimates are calculated and reported in terms of MW. Scale economics favour massive campuses that attract global operators, while government incentives and cross-border fiber links amplify Poland’s positioning as Central and Eastern Europe’s preferred interconnection hub. Competitive dynamics are shifting as hyperscalers begin to outpace local providers, and regional diversification beyond Warsaw accelerates to mitigate grid constraints.
